Meta Platforms (META) just announced a $6 billion deal with Corning (GLW) to supply a variety of components for data centers over the next several years. Anthropic is raising revenue projections by 20% to $18 billion in 2026, according to reports. Mordor Intelligence recently released a report suggesting that the data center market in Europe will expand by a CAGR of ~26% from 2026 to 2031. This makes sense given the increasing emphasis of reducing reliance on American tech companies. Nvidia reports accelerating AI spending in the financial services industry based on strong early returns.

For now, I expect AI spending to remain strong. A strained energy grid in the United States may encourage diversification, but APH has been generating more than half of its revenues from international markets. As we see below, APH has regularly beaten expectations in recent quarters. During the Q3 earnings report, APH’s guidance suggested that sales for Q4 would come in at $6 billion and $6.1 billion, but sales performed much better at $6.4 billion. As far as I can tell, the management team seems to set moderate expectations, making the positive forward guidance a bit more attractive in my eyes.

Why I Could Be Wrong

Amphenol’s products are more commoditized than say GPUs or AI software. I suspect we’ll see competition heat up rather quickly with connectors and cables, and this will likely occur even before the AI race cools off. If and when the AI data center build-out slows down, margins could come under immense pressure. I do think APH could be among the hardest hit stocks should the AI bubble pop, although defense applications may provide some cushion.

Key Takeaway: Sharp Dip Creates Favorable Entry Point, But Be Mindful Of Long-Term Risks

I believe most of the disappointment from overly exuberant investors should already be priced into APH. Further steep losses would be surprising, and there’s a good chance that there will be a moderate rebound. Data center build-out should remain brisk for now, and APH should continue to lock in strong sales. I do have concerns over margins, especially in the long-run, and believe we could see relatively intense competition in the components space in the not-so-distant future, so this is a stock and industry I recommend investors keep a close eye on.

data.title: Canadian Solar Boasts New AI Monetization Opportunity - Rerating May Be Slow
data.description:
I previously covered Canadian Solar (CSIQ) in October 2025, discussing how the company had benefitted from the lifting market sentiments, thanks to the recovering polysilicon spot prices and the likely higher solar module prices.

Despite that, I had believed that the stock was likely to underperform moving forward, attributed to the structural headwinds observed in the US solar sector, Recurring Energy's inherent lack of profitability, the bloated debt from the growing solar/energy storage assets/elevated U.S.-based capex, and the overbought technical indicators.

Since that last Hold rating, I shall discuss why I am finally re-rating CSIQ as a Buy, thanks to the new data center-related monetization opportunities as they leverage their solar/battery storage capabilities during the ongoing AI boom.

This is aided by the discounted valuations and the management highlighting a new 2026 focus on a healthier balance sheet, with the upward re-rating nearer to its power generation peers likely to trigger rich capital appreciation prospects.

CSIQ May Be an AI Beneficiary Ahead

Since my last Hold rating, the bulls have proven my bearish thesis wrong indeed, with numerous solar-related stocks rallying by the double digits since late October 2025, CSIQ included.

Their tailwinds are multifaceted, in my opinion, attributed to the Chinese government stepping in to "crack down on cut-throat competition and overcapacity in the solar manufacturing sector, as the industry struggles with a prolonged downturn."

The early success has been observed in the notable recovery in the global polysilicon price averages to $7.29 per kg by January 21, 2026, up by +30.8% from the $5.57 per kg observed in the July 2024 bottom - with it effectively marking the end of the solar price war, as similarly highlighted by a key polysilicon producer, Daqo New Energy (DQ), in the recent earnings call.

So with the recovery of market prices across the solar PV value chain in the third quarter of 2025, we believe the industry is gradually recovering from a cyclical downturn. In particular, the polysilicon sector reached an inflection point during the quarter with prices rebounding significantly. (Seeking Alpha)

This is significantly aided by the ongoing AI boom, with the aging utility infrastructure struggling to keep up with the hyperscalers' power-hungry data center build-outs, as numerous "data center developers estimate up to 2 years for grid power access."

Here is where numerous alternative power providers, including solar, nuclear, gas-powered utility plays, and on-site power generators, amongst others, have emerged as near-term beneficiaries, attributed to their supposedly faster deployment timelines and/or modular add-on capabilities and/or lower carbon footprints, as similarly reiterated by CSIQ in the last earnings call:

The rise of AI-driven data center is fueling unprecedented global electricity demand. As I have emphasized in my public speeches over the past 2 years, the most flexible and cost-effective solution for powering data centers is solar-plus-storage. In contrast, traditional energy sources such as natural gas and nuclear power require long construction cycles and have limited scalability. (Seeking Alpha)

Here is where CSIQ's vertically integrated offerings across...

  • CSI Solar, "focused on solar modules and battery energy storage manufacturing and products," and
  • Recurrent Energy, "focused on utility-scale solar power and battery energy storage project development and operation"

... have been able to benefit from the ongoing power "gold rush," as similarly observed in Recurrent Energy's growing total battery energy storage project development pipeline to 80.57 GWh in FQ3'25 (+21.8% YoY).

This is on top of the robust e-STORAGE contracted backlog, including contracted long-term service agreements, at $3.1B (-3.1% YoY), aided by the expanded Manufacturing Capacity Expansion Plans for SolBank Battery Energy Storage Solutions [BESS] to 24 GWh (+60% YoY) and Battery Cells to 9 GWh in 2026 (+200% YoY).

As a result of these promising developments and the excellent growth insights from the higher multi-year backlog, it is unsurprising that CSIQ has already rallied by +239.7% from the 52-week lows or by +426.4% between the 52-week lows/highs.

This is especially since the management has delivered operating discipline through the reduced expenses by -42.6% QoQ/-10.1% YoY in FQ3'25 to record a robust GAAP operating income margin of 2.3% in FQ3'25 (compared to 0.01% in FQ3'24), nearer to the 2.4% observed on a YTD basis (+0.3 points YoY).

Readers must note that CSIQ has also acknowledged its elevated debt leverage, as observed in their strategy in balancing "the growth of our operating portfolio and selective project ownership sales to prudently manage cash flow and debt levels," with 2026 expected to bring forth more "project ownership sales to enhance cash recycling."

This reason is also why readers may expect its total debt, including financing liabilities, to remain stable at or moderate from the $6.4B reported in FQ3'25 (+18.5% YoY), with the potentially reduced net interest expenses from the current levels of $29.34M (+43.5% YoY) likely to improve its bottom lines as well.

On the other hand, readers must note that CSIQ's project ownership sales and deleveraging process is likely to be lumpy and/or prolonged, with the solar company likely to remain unprofitable in the intermediate term, as observed in the adj EPS performance of -$0.58 in FQ3'25 (-87% YoY) and -$2.18 on a YTD basis (-2,080% YoY).

The same has been observed in the consensus forward estimates, with the consensus FY2026 GAAP EPS estimates of $1.00 likely to be negated by the annualized FQ3'25 preferred dividend impact at ~$0.80 per share.

Valuations Still Compelling Despite Recent Rally

For now, despite its lack of near-term profitability prospects, CSIQ remains compellingly valued at FWD EV/Sales valuations of 1.38x, compared to the sector median of 3.52x, despite the notable upgrade from the 5Y mean of 0.70x and the 10Y mean of 0.69x.

Given that the CSI manufacturing segment still comprises 95% of its overall FQ3'25 revenues and 6.6% of its income from operations (before eliminations), it is unsurprising that the stock remains inherently discounted compared to its Independent Power Producer [IPP] peers with more diversified power generation portfolios across solar, wind, nuclear, natural gas, storage, and others, including:

  • Brookfield Renewable Partners L.P. (BEP) at FWD EV/Sales valuations of 12.02x,
  • NextEra Energy, Inc. (NEE) at 9.81x, and
  • Clearway Energy, Inc. (CWEN) at 11.85x,

while being rated closer to its solar panel producer peers, including:

  • Jinko Solar at 0.76x,
  • Longi Green Energy Technology at 1.26x,
  • Tongwei at 1.44x,
  • GCL Technology Holdings (GCPEF) at 3.41x, and
  • First Solar (FSLR) at 4.87x.

This development implies CSIQ's discounted Recurrent Energy prospects - one that is somewhat deserved, since there remains a notable distance (approximately 4 years) until the segment becomes a top-line driver, where "until our IPP business scales further, near-term profitability will continue to depend primarily on global project sales."

As a result, I am of the opinion that the stock is likely to underperform expectations in the intermediate term, especially given baked-in exuberance from the recent rally.

CSIQ is Upgraded to a Cautious Buy

For now, CSIQ has lost much of its recent gains, albeit well defended at the November 2025/January 2026 bottom of $20s, with it implying a new trading floor if the buying momentum continues.

CSIQ Technical Indicators

This conjecture is not overly aggressive as well, based on the reasonable technical indicators across the RSIs and trading volumes, with it implying that CSIQ may chart a decent upside potential from current levels, assuming that the management beats the consensus forward estimates in the upcoming FQ4'25 earnings call sometime in March 2026.

For reference, the current FQ4'25 revenue estimates of $1.37B (-8% QoQ/-9.8% YoY) and adj. EPS estimates of -$0.90 (-55.1% QoQ/+38.77% YoY) imply a sequentially weaker performance, with the low bar implying a potentially outsized rally upon a top- and/or bottom-line beat, similar to the market's reaction post FQ3'25 earnings call.

Combined with the management hinting at their ongoing data center-related engagements across numerous customers in Spain and the US, I am of the opinion that CSIQ may be suitable for those seeking to tap into the multi-year data center/electrification growth trends.

As a result of the improved margin of safety from the growing solar/battery demand despite the changing US regulatory landscape, the correction of -35.9% from recent highs, and the established new trading floor at the $20s, I am cautiously upgrading the CSIQ stock as a Buy.

This is, albeit with the caveat that the stock is only suitable for those with a longer investing trajectory and a higher risk appetite, attributed to the Recurrent Energy segment's prolonged monetization opportunity and the elevated short interest ratio of 15.97% by the time of writing.
